front: What is steganography, specifically in the context of text?
back: Steganography is the practice of concealing a message, file, or information within another text file or cover text. Unlike cryptography, which obscures the content, steganography aims to hide the very existence of the communication.

front: What are two common techniques used for data hiding in text?
back: 1. Format-Based Methods: Manipulating white spaces (e.g., adding tabs/spaces at the end of lines), changing text formatting (font, color, size), or using zero-width characters. 2. Linguistic Methods: Using synonyms, paraphrasing, or generating semantically similar but lexically different text to encode information.

front: What are Zero-Width Characters (ZWCs) and how are they used in text steganography?
back: Zero-Width Characters are non-printing characters (e.g., Zero Width Joiner, Zero Width Non-Joiner) that take up no horizontal space. They can be embedded within regular text to encode binary data (e.g., 1 for ZWJ, 0 for ZWNJ) without altering the visible appearance of the text.

front: What is the key difference between steganography and cryptography?
back: Cryptography scrambles the content of a message to make it unreadable without a key, but the existence of a secret message is obvious. Steganography hides the very existence of the message within an innocuous-looking cover medium, so it is not obvious a secret is present.

front: What is a major challenge or drawback of text steganography?
back: A major drawback is low data hiding capacity (low bandwidth). Only a small amount of secret data can be embedded into a large amount of cover text without making the changes statistically detectable or visually suspicious to a human observer.
